one_line_takeaway: By splitting a diffusion watermark's semantic binding into four spatially separate partitions (subject/environment/action/detail) rather than one global code, SLICE cuts attack success under the CSI semantic-forgery attack from 81% (SEAL) to 19%, while remaining fragile to cropping-and-scaling attacks that it proposes patching with separate passive forensic detectors rather than fixing directly.
key_finding: Against generative forgery attacks (Table 1, Attack Success Rate %, lower is better): the three content-independent watermarks (Gaussian Shading, Tree-Ring, WIND) collapse to ASR of essentially 100% under LFA, RPM, and CSI (Tree-Ring is 93.81% under LFA, 100% under RPM and CSI; the other two are 100% across all three). SEAL (content-aware, prior SOTA) achieves 0% ASR under LFA and 7% under RPM but is broken by CSI at 81% ASR. SLICE achieves 0% ASR under LFA, 5% under RPM, and 19% under CSI (mean ASR across the three attacks: SLICE 8.0% vs. SEAL's per-attack values shown, with the paper reporting means of 58.76/62.40/80.00 for the three baseline-average rows). Robustness to benign perturbations (Table 2, detection accuracy): 1.000 clean, 1.000 rotation, 0.990 JPEG, 0.988 blur, 0.993 noise, 0.941 brightness. Fidelity (Table 3, CLIP score before/after watermarking): SDP 33.034 to 32.789; COCO 31.342 to 31.240 -- both small decreases. Case study (Section 6.4, tau_k=35): a State-I image has partition match counts subject 133/action 90/detail 111/environment 152 (all above threshold); after a gender/action edit, subject drops to 55, action to 26, detail to 33 (below threshold) while environment stays at 75, correctly yielding State II with the tampered factors identified; a wholly unrelated image yields subject 8/action 2/detail 18/environment 7, correctly triggering State III. Table 4 shows the acknowledged failure mode: under cropping-and-scaling, SLICE's own detection AUC falls to 0.054, while five separate passive-forensics detectors (feature stability 0.850, resampling 0.920, frequency-texture 0.940, ResNet18 0.980, spectral anisotropy 0.990) detect the same attack well, motivating a proposed joint active+passive defense.
core_contribution: SLICE (Semantic Latent Injection via Compartmentalized Embedding) is a training-free diffusion-model watermarking scheme that decomposes an image's semantics into four factors -- subject, environment, action, and detail -- via a VLM (Qwen3-VL) prompted with a fixed meta-prompt, and binds each factor to a distinct, non-overlapping spatial partition of the diffusion model's initial Gaussian noise latent using a keyed hash-based synthesis function H(E(s_k), p, sigma). At detection time the suspect image is DDIM-inverted to recover a latent, semantic descriptors are re-extracted with the same VLM/meta-prompt, a reference latent is reconstructed per-partition, and a pointwise distance d(p) is compared against per-partition thresholds tau_k and a global threshold tau_g, yielding a three-state verdict: (I) Authentic & Intact, (II) Localized Semantic Tampering (with the offending factor identified), or (III) Unwatermarked/Severely Corrupted. The core novelty relative to prior semantic-aware watermarking (specifically SEAL, which the same author group's own prior CSI attack paper broke) is replacing a single global semantic binding with four spatially compartmentalized bindings, so that a localized-but-globally-coherent semantic edit corrupts only the corresponding partition instead of leaving the whole watermark intact.
threat_model: The adversary generates or edits an image and wants a forged/tampered image to still pass watermark verification as authentic, without possessing the secret key sigma. Three concrete attacks from prior work are used to instantiate this: Latent Forgery Attack (LFA, Jain et al. 2025), Regeneration with a Private Model (RPM, from the SEAL/Arabi et al. line), and Coherence-Preserving Semantic Injection (CSI, Gao et al. 2026 -- an LLM-guided attack from the same author group that performs locally targeted but globally coherent semantic edits designed specifically to defeat global semantic-binding watermarks like SEAL). The defender controls the diffusion model, the secret key sigma used in the keyed hash H, and the VLM/meta-prompt used for semantic extraction; the verifier only needs the suspect image (no ground-truth original) plus the key. A secondary, non-adversarial threat considered is benign image degradation (rotation, JPEG, blur, noise, brightness) that should not trigger false rejections. The paper explicitly identifies a limitation: aggressive cropping-and-scaling (C&S) attacks defeat SLICE's active detection (AUC drops to 0.054) because they destroy the spatial correspondence needed for DDIM inversion; the paper proposes mitigating this by pairing SLICE with separate passive forensic detectors (feature-stability, resampling, frequency-texture, ResNet18, spectral-anisotropy methods) rather than by improving SLICE itself.
limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that SLICE's spatially-partitioned binding is fragile to aggressive cropping-and-scaling attacks (detection AUC collapses to 0.054) because such attacks break the spatial correspondence DDIM inversion relies on; their proposed fix is not an improvement to SLICE itself but bolting on unrelated passive forensic detectors as a second line of defense. The theoretical guarantees (Theorems 4.3, 4.4) rest on assumptions (bounded inversion error, stability/separation of semantic corruption, conditional independence of pointwise matches) that are not empirically validated against the reported epsilon/delta/rho/beta/gamma parameters in the experiments -- the paper does not report measured values for these constants. The prompt-language selection (Chinese) result is based on a single VLM (Qwen3-VL) and the paper offers only a post-hoc attribution ('stronger alignment... with Chinese prompts') without a mechanistic explanation, and does not test whether this generalizes to other VLMs. Watermark detection depends on a VLM being available and consistent at both embedding and verification time; VLM drift, API changes, or adversarial prompting of the VLM itself is not analyzed as a threat. All experiments use a single generator (Stable Diffusion V2); no evaluation is shown against more recent architectures (e.g., rectified-flow models) despite the paper noting these in the introduction. Only one CSI-attack ASR figure (19%) is reported without confidence intervals or seed variation.

quantitative_results: ASR under LFA: Gaussian Shading 100%, Tree-Ring 93.81%, WIND 100%, SEAL 0%, SLICE 0%
ASR under RPM: Gaussian Shading 100%, Tree-Ring 100%, WIND 100%, SEAL 7%, SLICE 5%
ASR under CSI: Gaussian Shading 100%, Tree-Ring 100%, WIND 100%, SEAL 81%, SLICE 19%
Mean ASR across LFA/RPM/CSI (per attack column, averaged over the four watermarks in the table): 58.76 / 62.40 / 80.00
Detection accuracy under perturbations: Clean 1.000, Rotate 1.000, JPEG 0.990, Blur 0.988, Noise 0.993, Brightness 0.941
CLIP score before/after watermarking on SDP: 33.034 -> 32.789
CLIP score before/after watermarking on COCO: 31.342 -> 31.240
Cropping-and-scaling attack: SLICE active-detection AUC drops to 0.054
Passive forensic mitigation AUCs against C&S: feature stability 0.850, resampling 0.920, frequency-texture 0.940, ResNet18 0.980, spectral anisotropy 0.990

Case study match counts (tau_k=35): State I image subject=133, action=90, detail=111, environment=152; State II (tampered) image subject=55, action=26, detail=33, environment=75; State III (unrelated) image subject=8, action=2, detail=18, environment=7
